findings.description: Top letterhead seal/logo block is misplaced and rotated in the HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the North Carolina seal and agency heading appear upright at the top center of the page. In the HTML, that graphic block appears upside down and embedded mid-page above the body text.

findings.description: Signature block formatting is substantially flattened.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, 'Sincerely,' is followed by a visible handwritten signature and a stacked signature/title block. In the HTML, this is reduced to plain inline text ('David Miller, PE State Mining Engineer'), losing visual distinction and separation.

findings.description: Reading order is disrupted by rotated content appearing before the main letter text.
findings.evidence: The PDF presents the letterhead and content in a clear top-to-bottom order. In the HTML, an upside-down duplicate/rotated section appears before the readable body text, causing a user to encounter content out of sequence.

findings.description: Overall page structure is not cleanly preserved due to inserted rotated duplicate content.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows a conventional centered letter layout with large whitespace and footer branding. The HTML includes an additional rotated block and compresses the content into a simpler continuous text layout.

findings.description: Handwritten signatures are not preserved as images/graphics in their original positions.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, Brian Wrenn's and Brenda Harris's signatures are clearly shown near their respective name lines. In the HTML, the page includes an upside-down image region and then plain text names without preserving the signatures in readable placement.

findings.description: Document title and signature/date formatting are degraded.
findings.evidence: The PDF has a centered, underlined, two-line title and distinct signed/date sections. The HTML reduces the title to plain uppercase text and flattens the signed sections into ordinary paragraphs, weakening document hierarchy.

findings.description: Reading order is disrupted by upside-down duplicated content placed above the readable text.
findings.evidence: The PDF begins with the notice title followed by body paragraphs and signatures. The HTML shows an upside-down rendered page segment first, then repeats the content in readable text below, causing sequence confusion.

findings.description: Whitespace groupings and section separation are compressed in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses spacing to separate paragraphs, signature blocks, and mailing date. The HTML presents these sections in a tighter continuous flow with less visual separation.

findings.description: Heading structure does not accurately reflect the document structure. The main visible document heading is marked as an h3, and there is no h1/h2 representing the document title or section hierarchy.
findings.evidence:
PDF page 2 shows the prominent centered title "NOTICE OF ISSUANCE OF MINING PERMIT" as the primary heading. HTML renders this as "

NOTICE OF ISSUANCE OF MINING PERMIT

" with no higher-level heading present.

findings.description: Alt text is present but generic rather than descriptive of the actual scanned content.
findings.evidence: HTML uses alt text "Page 1 Scanned Document" and "Page 2 Scanned Document" for the two page images. These do not describe the visible content beyond page identity.
